reason:
Both answers correctly identify the core reposition: drop "AI-powered PM," lead with resource/utilization forecasting, narrow the competitive set to Float/Runn-type tools instead of the 30-way PM war, shift the buyer to P&L/ops leadership, and treat PM features as supporting context rather than the pitch — with land-on-forecasting-then-expand as the go-to-market path. Neither has factual or reasoning errors, and both are similarly concise and concrete (including demo advice and competitor names).

B edges ahead on marginal usefulness: it adds a billable-hours-based pricing anchor (vs. seats), a low-friction "works alongside your existing PM tool" wedge to ease switching costs, and a crisp, falsifiable self-check ("if a prospect describes you as 'like Monday but with AI,' positioning failed") that gives the asker a concrete way to validate success. A is well-organized and hits the same key points but doesn't offer anything beyond what B covers equally well.
